title: Carta esférica de los reconocimientos hechos en 1792 en la costa N.O. de América
cartographer: Dionisio Alcalá Galiano & Cayetano Valdés (survey); pub. Dirección Hidrográfica
in: Relación del viage hecho por las goletas Sutil y Mexicana (Madrid, 1802)
source_repository: David Rumsey Map Collection / Internet Archive
license: public domain; attribution to source repository
headnote: The coast as Spain itself charted it, from the 1792 survey of the schooners Sutil and Mexicana — the cartographic record of the very empire whose San Blas supply fleet is the opening act of this registry's traffic curve. Published in the 1802 official Relación, it is the Spanish-hand map contemporary with the founding decades, and the same source the companion Bodega study reads for the coast's legal geography.
look_for: the still-tentative rendering of the northern anchorages Spain claimed but rarely visited — Bodega and the coast above San Francisco.
verify: CONFIRMED — David Rumsey plate on Internet Archive, IIIF-enabled (georeference in Allmaps via the manifest)

title: A Chart shewing part of the Coast of N.W. America [New Albion / California]
in: A Voyage of Discovery to the North Pacific Ocean and Round the World (London, 1798)
source_repository: David Rumsey Map Collection / Internet Archive
license: public domain; attribution to source repository
headnote: The outside power charting a coast Spain claimed — Vancouver's survey of 1792–94, made in the very Nootka-crisis decade this registry shows the door opening. His three California calls are themselves rows in the data; his chart is the map of that moment, drawn by the visitor rather than the sovereign.
look_for: the accuracy of the British survey against the Spanish Sutil chart of the same coast — the rival's map is often the better one.
verify: CONFIRMED atlas (voyageofdiscover02vanc, vol.2 1798); locate the California/New Albion plate within, or the dr_ single-sheet

title: Carte de la Côte de l'Amérique sur l'Océan Pacifique Nord … Territoire de l'Orégon, les Californies
short_title: Duflot de Mofras, Côte de l'Amérique
in: Exploration du territoire de l'Orégon, des Californies et de la mer Vermeille (Paris, 1844)
region_served: Sitka to San Blas — every anchorage
license: public domain; © David Rumsey Map Collection (attribution required)
headnote: The default basemap: the late-Mexican coast on one detailed sheet, from a French diplomatic-scientific survey on the eve of the American conquest. It covers every anchorage in the registry and is the natural ground for the hide-and-tallow decades — the licensed-door era when the traffic curve tips from seizure to customs. Companion to the vault's Duflot Bodega roadstead plan.
look_for: the named coastal establishments and soundings that show how thoroughly foreigners knew a coast Mexico could barely garrison.

title: Plano del Puerto de San Francisco
cartographer: José de Cañizares (pilot), Ayala expedition, San Carlos
license: public domain; attribution to source repository
headnote: The first Spanish chart of San Francisco Bay, drawn the year the San Carlos first entered it — the harbour every ship of the registry's opening act would come to know. A port inset for the Spanish-era San Francisco visits.
look_for: the survey's first, exploratory soundings of a bay no European had charted before.

title: Plan of the Harbour of San Francisco and its Vicinity
in: Narrative of a Voyage to the Pacific and Beering's Strait (London, 1831)
license: public domain; attribution to source repository
headnote: The definitive chart of the bay, surveyed by Beechey's Blossom in the very decade San Francisco filled with the hide-trade fleet — the natural inset for the registry's 1820s–40s San Francisco visits. The Blossom's own 1826 call is a row in the data.
look_for: the anchorage grounds off Yerba Buena where the Boston droghers rode — the geography of the licensed-door trade.
verify: CONFIRMED — David Rumsey plate on Internet Archive, IIIF-enabled

title: Chart of the Bay of San Francisco (U.S. Exploring Expedition)
license: public domain; attribution to source repository
headnote: The American survey of San Francisco Bay on the eve of conquest — the bay the U.S. Navy would take five years later, charted by the Wilkes expedition. The Act-V inset, where the flag begins to follow the trade the registry has tracked for seventy years.
look_for: the American naming and soundings that anticipate the harbour of the Gold Rush.
verify: atlas narrativeofunite01wilk_0; locate the SF Bay chart plate (Ringgold survey) within

title: Mouillage de la Bodega
license: public domain; © David Rumsey Map Collection (attribution required)
headnote: The roadstead the whole Bodega thesis turns on, charted by Duflot in 1844 as the Russians were withdrawing — the anchorage Spain ordered occupied in 1793 and never held. Already in the vault; georeference via Allmaps as the Bodega port inset.
look_for: how shallow and exposed the 'port' actually is — the geographic objection the Bodega study answers.
verify: the vault scan is maps/collection/duflot-1844-fort-ross-bodega-mouillage.jpg; confirm the exact Rumsey plate ID (maps6326 is the Mazatlán plate, NOT this one)
